This interface abstracts over different\n * versions of the record format (magic versions 0, 1, 2, and higher) and provides access to batch metadata\n * and individual records.\n *\n * <p><strong>Record Format Versions:</strong>\n * <ul>\n *   <li><strong>Magic V0 & V1:</strong> Older formats where a batch could contain a single record (uncompressed)\n *       or multiple records (compressed). These versions lack producer ID, sequence numbers, and timestamps.</li>\n *   <li><strong>Magic V2+:</strong> Newer formats that support idempotent and transactional producers with sequence numbers,\n *       producer ID/epoch tracking, and explicit timestamp types. These batches support both compressed and\n *       uncompressed records.</li>\n * </ul>\n *\n * <p><strong>Role in Kafka Protocol:</strong>\n * Record batches are the basic unit of data in Kafka logs. They encapsulate a group of records with shared metadata\n * (compression type, timestamp type, producer ID, etc.) and provide efficient encoding of records using offset/timestamp\n * deltas relative to batch base values. This design minimizes storage overhead when records share similar offsets and timestamps.\n *\n * <p><strong>Compaction Behavior:</strong>\n * During log compaction, base and last offset/sequence numbers are preserved for V2+ batches to maintain producer\n * state consistency, but individual records may be removed. This allows brokers to rebuild producer state from the log.\n *\n * <p><strong>Thread Safety:</strong>\n * This interface does not mandate thread-safety. Implementations may be mutable (e.g., {@link DefaultRecordBatch})\n * and should not be accessed concurrently from multiple threads without external synchronization.\n *\n * @see DefaultRecordBatch for the V2+ concrete implementation\n * @see MemoryRecords for in-memory collections of record batches\n */\npublic interface RecordBatch extends Iterable<Record> {\n\n    /**\n     * The \"magic\" values\n     */\n    byte MAGIC_VALUE_V0 = 0;\n    byte MAGIC_VALUE_V1 = 1;\n    byte MAGIC_VALUE_V2 = 2;\n\n    /**\n     * The current \"magic\" value\n     */\n    byte CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE = MAGIC_VALUE_V2;\n\n    /**\n     * Timestamp value for records without a timestamp\n     */\n    long NO_TIMESTAMP = -1L;\n\n    /**\n     * Values used in the v2 record format by non-idempotent/non-transactional producers or when\n     * up-converting from an older format.\n     */","numLines":80,"startLine":1,"totalLines":299}}}

44→/**\n    45→ * In-memory collection of Kafka record batches backed by a ByteBuffer.\n    46→ *\n    47→ * <p><strong>Purpose:</strong>\n    48→ * This class manages a contiguous buffer containing one or more {@link RecordBatch} objects,\n    49→ * providing iteration, filtering, slicing, and serialization capabilities. It is used throughout\n    50→ * Kafka for handling batches in producers, consumers, brokers, and administrative operations.\n    51→ *\n    52→ * <p><strong>Use Cases:</strong>\n    53→ * <ul>\n    54→ *   <li><strong>Reading Existing Batches:</strong> Use {@link #readableRecords(ByteBuffer)} to wrap\n    55→ *       an existing buffer for reading.</li>\n    56→ *   <li><strong>Creating New Batches:</strong> Use {@link #builder(ByteBuffer, Compression, TimestampType, long)}\n    57→ *       or {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder} for batch construction.</li>\n    58→ *   <li><strong>Log Filtering & Compaction:</strong> Use {@link #filterTo(RecordFilter, ByteBuffer, BufferSupplier)}\n    59→ *       to filter records by retention policy during log compaction.</li>\n    60→ *   <li><strong>Network I/O:</strong> Use {@link #writeTo(java.nio.channels.GatheringByteChannel)} or\n    61→ *       {@link #writeTo(java.nio.channels.TransferableChannel, int, int)} for efficient data transmission.</li>\n    62→ * </ul>\n    63→ *\n    64→ * <p><strong>Batch Structure:</strong>\n    65→ * The buffer contains a sequence of batches (one or more). Each batch encapsulates records with shared\n    66→ * metadata (compression type, timestamp type, producer ID, etc.). Batches are decoded lazily as they are\n    67→ * iterated; use {@link #batches()} to iterate over all batches or individual methods like\n    68→ * {@link #firstBatchSize()} to inspect specific batches.\n    69→ *\n    70→ * <p><strong>Decompression & Iterator Semantics:</strong>\n    71→ * <ul>\n    72→ *   <li><strong>Streaming Iteration:</strong> {@link RecordBatch#streamingIterator(BufferSupplier)}\n    73→ *       defers decompression until records are consumed, reducing memory footprint.</li>\n    74→ *   <li><strong>Regular Iteration:</strong> {@link RecordBatch#iterator()} decompresses the full batch\n    75→ *       upfront, allowing iteration without resource management concerns.</li>\n    76→ *   <li><strong>Filtering:</strong> {@link #filterTo(RecordFilter, ByteBuffer, BufferSupplier)} combines\n    77→ *       batch and record filtering, supporting log compaction and retention policies.</li>\n    78→ * </ul>\n    79→ *\n    80→ * <p><strong>Thread Safety:</strong>\n    81→ * This class is NOT thread-safe. The underlying ByteBuffer is accessed without synchronization.\n    82→ * Multiple threads must not read or write the same MemoryRecords instance concurrently.\n    83→ * For concurrent access, wrap each thread's iteration with external synchronization or use\n    84→ * thread-local instances.\n    85→ *\n    86→ * <p><strong>Buffer Ownership & Lifecycle:</strong>\n    87→ * MemoryRecords does not own the backing ByteBuffer; the caller is responsible for:\n    88→ * <ul>\n    89→ *   <li>Keeping the buffer valid during MemoryRecords' lifetime</li>\n    90→ *   <li>Releasing/deallocating the buffer when no longer needed</li>\n    91→ *   <li>Avoiding modifications to the buffer while iteration is in progress</li>\n    92→ * </ul>\n    93→ *\n    94→ * <p><strong>Performance Characteristics:</strong>\n    95→ * <ul>\n    96→ *   <li><strong>Lazy Batch Parsing:</strong> Batches are decoded on-demand during iteration.</li>\n    97→ *   <li><strong>Valid Bytes Caching:</strong> {@link #validBytes()} is computed once and cached;\n    98→ *       invalidate by creating a new MemoryRecords instance if the buffer changes.</li>\n    99→ *   <li><strong>Decompression Buffers:</strong> Provide reusable {@link BufferSupplier} instances\n   100→ *       to streaming iterators to reduce allocations for large compressed batches.</li>\n   101→ * </ul>\n   102→ *\n   103→ * @see RecordBatch for the batch interface\n   104→ * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder for constructing new MemoryRecords\n   105→ * @see DefaultRecordBatch for the V2+ batch implementation\n   106→ */\n   107→public class MemoryRecords extends AbstractRecords {\n   108→    public static final MemoryRecords EMPTY = MemoryRecords.readableRecords(ByteBuffer.allocate(0));\n   109→\n   110→    private final ByteBuffer buffer;\n   111→\n   112→    private final Iterable<MutableRecordBatch> batches = this::batchIterator;\n   113→\n   114→    private int validBytes = -1;\n   115→\n   116→    // Construct a writable memory records\n   117→    private MemoryRecords(ByteBuffer buffer) {\n   118→        Objects.requireNonNull(buffer, \"buffer should not be null\");\n   119→        this.buffer = buffer;\n   120→    }\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. It is used throughout\n * Kafka for handling batches in producers, consumers, brokers, and administrative operations.\n *\n * <p><strong>Use Cases:</strong>\n * <ul>\n *   <li><strong>Reading Existing Batches:</strong> Use {@link #readableRecords(ByteBuffer)} to wrap\n *       an existing buffer for reading.</li>\n *   <li><strong>Creating New Batches:</strong> Use {@link #builder(ByteBuffer, Compression, TimestampType, long)}\n *       or {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder} for batch construction.</li>\n *   <li><strong>Log Filtering & Compaction:</strong> Use {@link #filterTo(RecordFilter, ByteBuffer, BufferSupplier)}\n *       to filter records by retention policy during log compaction.</li>\n *   <li><strong>Network I/O:</strong> Use {@link #writeTo(java.nio.channels.GatheringByteChannel)} or\n *       {@link #writeTo(java.nio.channels.TransferableChannel, int, int)} for efficient data transmission.</li>\n * </ul>\n *\n * <p><strong>Batch Structure:</strong>\n * The buffer contains a sequence of batches (one or more). Each batch encapsulates records with shared\n * metadata (compression type, timestamp type, producer ID, etc.). Batches are decoded lazily as they are\n * iterated; use {@link #batches()} to iterate over all batches or individual methods like\n * {@link #firstBatchSize()} to inspect specific batches.\n *\n * <p><strong>Decompression & Iterator Semantics:</strong>\n * <ul>\n *   <li><strong>Streaming Iteration:</strong> {@link RecordBatch#streamingIterator(BufferSupplier)}\n *       defers decompression until records are consumed, reducing memory footprint.</li>\n *   <li><strong>Regular Iteration:</strong> {@link RecordBatch#iterator()} decompresses the full batch\n *       upfront, allowing iteration without resource management concerns.</li>\n *   <li><strong>Filtering:</strong> {@link #filterTo(RecordFilter, ByteBuffer, BufferSupplier)} combines\n *       batch and record filtering, supporting log compaction and retention policies.</li>\n * </ul>\n *\n * <p><strong>Thread Safety:</strong>\n * This class is NOT thread-safe. The underlying ByteBuffer is accessed without synchronization.\n * Multiple threads must not read or write the same MemoryRecords instance concurrently.\n * For concurrent access, wrap each thread's iteration with external synchronization or use\n * thread-local instances.\n *\n * <p><strong>Buffer Ownership & Lifecycle:</strong>\n * MemoryRecords does not own the backing ByteBuffer; the caller is responsible for:\n * <ul>\n *   <li>Keeping the buffer valid during MemoryRecords' lifetime</li>\n *   <li>Releasing/deallocating the buffer when no longer needed</li>\n *   <li>Avoiding modifications to the buffer while iteration is in progress</li>\n * </ul>\n *\n * <p><strong>Performance Characteristics:</strong>\n * <ul>\n *   <li><strong>Lazy Batch Parsing:</strong> Batches are decoded on-demand during iteration.</li>\n *   <li><strong>Valid Bytes Caching:</strong> {@link #validBytes()} is computed once and cached;\n *       invalidate by creating a new MemoryRecords instance if the buffer changes.</li>\n *   <li><strong>Decompression Buffers:</strong> Provide reusable {@link BufferSupplier} instances\n *       to streaming iterators to reduce allocations for large compressed batches.</li>\n * </ul>\n *\n * @see RecordBatch for the batch interface\n * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder for constructing new MemoryRecords\n * @see DefaultRecordBatch for the V2+ batch implementation\n */\npublic class MemoryRecords extends AbstractRecords {\n    public static final MemoryRecords EMPTY = MemoryRecords.readableRecords(ByteBuffer.allocate(0));\n\n    private final ByteBuffer buffer;\n\n    private final Iterable<MutableRecordBatch> batches = this::batchIterator;\n\n    private int validBytes = -1;\n\n    // Construct a writable memory records\n    private MemoryRecords(ByteBuffer buffer) {\n        Objects.requireNonNull(buffer, \"buffer should not be null\");\n        this.buffer = buffer;\n    }","numLines":120,"startLine":1,"totalLines":1108}}}
